knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse   = TRUE,
  comment    = "#>",
  fig.width  = 7,
  fig.height = 5,
  message    = FALSE,
  warning    = FALSE
)
run_heavy <- identical(Sys.getenv("RAJIVEPLUS_VIGNETTE_HEAVY"), "1")
data_dir  <- "data"

The question

What can you ask of a fitted RaJIVE model? This gallery walks through the exported user-facing functions in rajiveplus using one small three-block simulation. The example is deliberately light so the whole tour can run quickly; for a biological analysis, see vignette("cll_application").

Each stop follows the same rhythm: fit or extract one object, inspect the output, then say what scientific question the output can answer.

Roadmap

Category Functions
Simulation ajive.data.sim
Core decomposition Rajive
S3 — rajive class print, summary
Rank accessors get_joint_rank, get_individual_rank, get_all_ranks
Component extractors get_joint_scores, get_block_scores, get_block_loadings, get_block_matrix
Diagnostic extraction extract_components (5 what modes)
Decomposition visualization data_heatmap, decomposition_heatmaps_robustH
Variance explained joint_variance_partition, plot_variance_explained, showVarExplained_robust
Score plots plot_scores, plot_components (pairs, density)
AJIVE diagnostic plots plot_components (rank_threshold, bound_distributions, ajive_diagnostic)
Feature visualization plot_components (top_features, component_heatmap)
S3 autoplot / fortify autoplot, fortify
Feature ranking rank_features (4 modes) + 3 wrappers
Association testing associate_components (4 modes) + 3 wrappers; plot_components (association)
Stability assess_stability (3 targets) + 2 wrappers; plot_stability_heatmap; plot_components (stability)
Summarization summarize_components (6 summary_type values)
Jackstraw jackstraw_rajive; print, summary, autoplot, fortify; plot_jackstraw (3 types); plot_components (volcano, jackstraw_summary); get_significant_vars; summarize_significant_vars
Reporting / export export_results, rajive_report

Prerequisites

library(rajiveplus)
library(ggplot2)

Shared Simulated Data

# ===== Run once (run_heavy = TRUE) to generate the gallery cache =====
set.seed(2)
n   <- 80L
pks <- c(60L, 50L, 40L)

sim      <- ajive.data.sim(K = 3, rankJ = 2, rankA = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                           n = n, pks = pks, dist.type = 1)
blocks   <- sim$sim_data
names(blocks) <- c("mRNA", "Methylation", "Proteomics")
for (k in seq_along(blocks))
  rownames(blocks[[k]]) <- paste0("sample", seq_len(n))

meta_df <- data.frame(
  group      = rep(c("A", "B"), each = n / 2L),
  score_cont = rnorm(n),
  time_event = abs(rnorm(n, mean = 5, sd = 2)),
  event_flag = rbinom(n, 1L, 0.6),
  row.names  = paste0("sample", seq_len(n)),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

fit <- Rajive(
  blocks               = blocks,
  initial_signal_ranks = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
  n_wedin_samples      = 200L,
  n_rand_dir_samples   = 200L,
  seed                 = 2L
)

set.seed(42)
js <- jackstraw_rajive(fit, blocks, n_null = 5L, correction = "BH")

assoc_cat  <- associate_components(fit, meta_df, variable = "group",
                                   mode = "categorical")
assoc_cont <- associate_components(fit, meta_df, variable = "score_cont",
                                   mode = "continuous")

stab_rank <- assess_stability(fit, blocks, c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                              target = "joint_rank", B = 5L,
                              seed = 12L)
stab_load <- assess_stability(fit, blocks, c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                              target = "loadings",   B = 5L,
                              seed = 13L)
stab_comp <- assess_stability(fit, blocks, c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                              target = "components", B = 5L,
                              seed = 14L)

dir.create(data_dir, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)
saveRDS(
  list(blocks = blocks, meta = meta_df, fit = fit, js = js,
       assoc_cat = assoc_cat, assoc_cont = assoc_cont,
       stab_rank = stab_rank, stab_load = stab_load, stab_comp = stab_comp),
  file = file.path(data_dir, "gallery_precomp.rds")
)
# ===== Load cached results (always runs) =====
cache      <- readRDS(file.path(data_dir, "gallery_precomp.rds"))
blocks     <- cache$blocks
meta_df    <- cache$meta
fit        <- cache$fit
js         <- cache$js
assoc_cat  <- cache$assoc_cat
assoc_cont <- cache$assoc_cont
stab_rank  <- cache$stab_rank
stab_load  <- cache$stab_load
stab_comp  <- cache$stab_comp

1 Data Simulation

ajive.data.sim()

Synthetic data give us a model with known structure. That makes it possible to check whether a function is recovering the planted joint and individual ranks rather than merely producing plausible-looking output.

set.seed(7)
sim2 <- ajive.data.sim(
  K         = 2,
  rankJ     = 2,
  rankA     = c(4, 3),
  n         = 60,
  pks       = c(80, 60),
  dist.type = 1,
  noise     = 1
)
cat("True joint rank:      ", sim2$rankJ_true, "\n")
#> True joint rank:
cat("True individual ranks:", sim2$rankA_true, "\n")
#> True individual ranks:
cat("Block 1 dimensions:   ", dim(sim2$sim_data[[1]]), "\n")
#> Block 1 dimensions:    60 80

What to look for. sim_data is a list of K sample-by-feature matrices. rankJ_true and rankA_true record the planted ranks, which become the truth set for recovery checks.

2 Core Decomposition

Rajive()

Rajive() is the central decomposition. It separates each block into shared joint structure, block-specific individual structure, and residual noise.

fit_demo <- Rajive(
  blocks               = blocks,
  initial_signal_ranks = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
  full                 = TRUE,
  n_wedin_samples      = 200L,
  n_rand_dir_samples   = 200L,
  joint_rank           = NA,
  num_cores            = 1L,
  seed                 = 2L
)

What to look for. The result is a rajive S3 object. Use joint_rank = NA when the rank should be estimated. Choose initial_signal_ranks conservatively above the expected signal rank in each block, because this is the search space from which joint structure is found.

Rajive() — permutation-based rank selection (n_perm_samples)

What it changes

The default rank-selection pipeline combines two bounds:

  1. Wedin bound — resamples row-subsets of each block’s SVD to estimate the perturbation threshold on the joint singular values.
  2. Random-direction bound — projects random Gaussian vectors through the signal subspaces to characterise the null distribution parametrically.

n_perm_samples adds a non-parametric alternative to the random-direction step. In each draw, every block’s signal scores are row-shuffled, the blocks are stacked, and the leading squared singular value is recorded. The 95th percentile becomes the permutation threshold.

When to use it

Scenario Recommendation
Data are approximately Gaussian Default (n_rand_dir_samples) is faster and equally valid
Heavy-tailed, count, or compositional data n_perm_samples adapts to the actual noise distribution
Strong batch effects within blocks n_perm_samples captures inter-block confounding that Gaussian null may miss
Quick exploratory runs n_rand_dir_samples is cheaper per sample

Usage

Set n_perm_samples to a positive integer and leave n_rand_dir_samples = NA (or any value — permutation takes precedence when both are set).

set.seed(42L)
fit_perm <- Rajive(
  blocks               = blocks,
  initial_signal_ranks = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
  full                 = TRUE,
  n_wedin_samples      = 200L,
  n_perm_samples       = 200L,   # replaces random-direction bound
  joint_rank           = NA,
  num_cores            = 1L,
  seed                 = 42L
)

# Inspect the permutation null
perm_info <- fit_perm$joint_rank_sel$perm
cat("Permutation threshold (95th pctile sq SV):",
    round(perm_info$perm_svsq_threshold, 4), "\n")
#> Permutation threshold (95th pctile sq SV): 1.7731
cat("Combined threshold (max wedin, perm):      ",
    round(fit_perm$joint_rank_sel$overall_sv_sq_threshold, 4), "\n")
#> Combined threshold (max wedin, perm):       1.7731
cat("Estimated joint rank:", fit_perm$joint_rank, "\n")
#> Estimated joint rank: 2

What to look for. fit_perm$joint_rank_sel$perm contains two elements: perm_samples (the vector of n_perm_samples squared singular values) and perm_svsq_threshold (the 95th percentile used as the null cut-off). The combined threshold is max(wedin_threshold, perm_threshold) — a conservative intersection that requires the observed signal to exceed both independent criteria.

Visualising the permutation null

# Diagnostic plot: shows obs sq-SVs against both Wedin and permutation thresholds
plot_components(fit_perm, plot_type = "ajive_diagnostic")

The diagnostic panel puts the decision on one page:

  • Observed squared singular values (dots) with the rank cut-off (vertical dashed line).
  • Wedin null distribution (blue histogram, lower left) — resampled from data geometry.
  • Permutation null distribution (green histogram, lower right) — derived by row-shuffling signal scores.

The dashed vertical line in each histogram marks the corresponding 95th-percentile threshold; the overall cut-off (dashed red line on the SV plot) is their maximum.

Reproducibility

Permutation draws use R’s global RNG. Set set.seed() before calling Rajive() when the draws must be reproducible. The samples are also stored in fit$joint_rank_sel$perm$perm_samples, so the null can be inspected later without refitting.

3 S3 Methods — rajive class

print.rajive()

print(fit)
#> RaJIVE Decomposition
#>   Number of blocks : 3
#>   Joint rank       : 2
#>   Individual ranks : 3, 2, 2

What to look for. The print method gives the number of blocks, the estimated joint rank, and the individual rank per block. A joint rank of 0 means the fit did not detect shared signal.

summary.rajive()

summary(fit)
#>   block joint_rank individual_rank
#>  block1          2               3
#>  block2          2               2
#>  block3          2               2

What to look for. The summary is a tidy one-row-per-block table. It is the quick sanity check before extracting scores, loadings, or matrices.

4 Rank Accessors

get_joint_rank()

jrank <- get_joint_rank(fit)
cat("Estimated joint rank:", jrank, "\n")
#> Estimated joint rank: 2

get_individual_rank()

for (k in seq_along(blocks))
  cat(sprintf("Block %d (%s) individual rank: %d\n",
              k, names(blocks)[k], get_individual_rank(fit, k)))
#> Block 1 (mRNA) individual rank: 3
#> Block 2 (Methylation) individual rank: 2
#> Block 3 (Proteomics) individual rank: 2

get_all_ranks()

rank_tbl <- get_all_ranks(fit)
knitr::kable(rank_tbl, caption = "All block ranks")
All block ranks
block joint_rank individual_rank
block1 2 3
block2 2 2
block3 2 2

What to look for. This combines the shared rank and each block’s individual rank into one reportable table.

5 Component Extractors

get_joint_scores()

J_scores <- get_joint_scores(fit)
cat("Dimensions (samples x joint components):", dim(J_scores), "\n")
#> Dimensions (samples x joint components): 80 2
print(round(head(J_scores, 4), 3))
#>        [,1]   [,2]
#> [1,] -0.126  0.036
#> [2,]  0.093 -0.128
#> [3,]  0.141  0.069
#> [4,]  0.028 -0.171

What to look for. Each column is one joint factor and each row is one sample. These scores are estimated, so downstream association p-values should be read as exploratory unless uncertainty is propagated.

get_block_scores()

bl1_joint  <- get_block_scores(fit, k = 1, type = "joint")
bl2_indiv  <- get_block_scores(fit, k = 2, type = "individual")
cat("Block 1 joint scores:", dim(bl1_joint), "\n")
#> Block 1 joint scores: 80 2
cat("Block 2 individual scores:", dim(bl2_indiv), "\n")
#> Block 2 individual scores: 80 2

get_block_loadings()

bl1_load <- get_block_loadings(fit, k = 1, type = "joint")
cat("Block 1 joint loadings (features x components):", dim(bl1_load), "\n")
#> Block 1 joint loadings (features x components): 60 2

What to look for. Large absolute loadings mark features aligned with the component. For feature-level significance, use jackstraw_rajive() rather than the raw loading rank alone.

get_block_matrix()

J1 <- get_block_matrix(fit, k = 1, type = "joint")
I1 <- get_block_matrix(fit, k = 1, type = "individual")
E1 <- get_block_matrix(fit, k = 1, type = "noise")
cat("Reconstruction error:", round(max(abs(blocks[[1]] - J1 - I1 - E1)), 6), "\n")
#> Reconstruction error: 0

What to look for. The joint, individual, and noise matrices reconstruct the original block. The split lets you ask whether a pattern is shared across blocks or belongs mainly to one block.

6 Diagnostic Extraction

extract_components() — what = "rank_diagnostics" (wide)

diag_wide <- extract_components(fit, what = "rank_diagnostics", format = "wide")
cat("Cutoff rule:", diag_wide$cutoff_rule, "\n")
#> Cutoff rule: max(wedin, random)
cat("Wedin threshold:", round(diag_wide$wedin_cutoff, 4), "\n")
#> Wedin threshold: -26.4289
cat("Rand-dir threshold:", round(diag_wide$rand_cutoff, 4), "\n")
#> Rand-dir threshold: 1.7367
cat("Joint rank estimate:", diag_wide$joint_rank_estimate, "\n")
#> Joint rank estimate: 2

What to look for. The overall threshold is max(wedin_cutoff, rand_cutoff). It is a useful empirical rule, not a formal FWER or FDR guarantee.

extract_components() — what = "rank_diagnostics" (long)

diag_long <- extract_components(fit, what = "rank_diagnostics", format = "long")
knitr::kable(head(diag_long, 6),
             caption = "Rank diagnostics — long format (first 6 SVs)")
Rank diagnostics — long format (first 6 SVs)
component_index obs_sval obs_sval_sq classification joint_rank_estimate overall_sv_sq_threshold wedin_cutoff rand_cutoff perm_cutoff identifiability_norm
1 1.653121 2.732808 joint 2 1.736679 -26.42887 1.736679 NA l2
2 1.606705 2.581503 joint 2 1.736679 -26.42887 1.736679 NA l2
3 1.171771 1.373047 nonjoint 2 1.736679 -26.42887 1.736679 NA l2
4 1.125616 1.267010 nonjoint 2 1.736679 -26.42887 1.736679 NA l2

extract_components() — what = "scores"

scores_long <- extract_components(fit, what = "scores", type = "joint",
                                  format = "long")
cat("Columns:", names(scores_long), "\n")
#> Columns: block type component sample score
head(scores_long, 4)
#>   block  type component sample       score
#> 1     1 joint         1      1 -0.12610387
#> 2     1 joint         1      2  0.09265652
#> 3     1 joint         1      3  0.14098029
#> 4     1 joint         1      4  0.02770708

extract_components() — what = "loadings"

load_long <- extract_components(fit, what = "loadings", type = "joint",
                                block = 1L, format = "long")
cat("Loadings dimensions:", dim(load_long), "\n")
#> Loadings dimensions: 120 5
head(load_long, 4)
#>   block  type component feature      loading
#> 1     1 joint         1       1 -0.096334898
#> 2     1 joint         1       2 -0.006311109
#> 3     1 joint         1       3  0.081390685
#> 4     1 joint         1       4 -0.331335060

extract_components() — what = "variance"

var_df <- extract_components(fit, blocks = blocks, what = "variance")
knitr::kable(round(var_df, 3), caption = "Variance explained per block (%)")
Variance explained per block (%)
Joint Indiv Resid
0.321 0.438 0.241
0.334 0.367 0.299
0.271 0.471 0.258

7 Decomposition Visualization

data_heatmap() — requires reshape2

if (requireNamespace("reshape2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  data_heatmap(
    data  = t(blocks[[1]][, 1:20]),
    title = "Block 1 (mRNA): first 20 features",
    xlab  = "Samples",
    ylab  = "Features"
  )
}

What to look for. Coherent color bands across samples point to latent structure before decomposition.

decomposition_heatmaps_robustH() — requires cowplot + reshape2

if (requireNamespace("cowplot", quietly = TRUE) &&
    requireNamespace("reshape2", quietly = TRUE)) {
  decomposition_heatmaps_robustH(blocks = blocks, jive_results_robust = fit)
}

What to look for. The four panels separate original, joint, individual, and noise signal. Shared structure should appear in the joint panels across blocks; the noise panels should be comparatively featureless.

8 Variance Explained

joint_variance_partition()

vp <- joint_variance_partition(fit, blocks)
knitr::kable(head(vp[, c("block", "feature", "joint_prop",
                         "individual_prop", "residual_prop")]),
             digits = 3,
             caption = "Feature-level variance partition")
Feature-level variance partition
block feature joint_prop individual_prop residual_prop
mRNA feature1 0.163 0.681 0.155
mRNA feature2 0.005 0.705 0.290
mRNA feature3 0.685 0.130 0.185
mRNA feature4 0.791 0.083 0.125
mRNA feature5 0.384 0.284 0.333
mRNA feature6 0.223 0.624 0.153

showVarExplained_robust()

This compatibility helper returns the historical block-level variance list. Use joint_variance_partition() when feature-level accounting is needed.

var_list <- showVarExplained_robust(fit, blocks)
cat("Joint:     ", round(unlist(var_list$Joint), 3), "\n")
#> Joint:      0.321 0.334 0.271
cat("Individual:", round(unlist(var_list$Indiv), 3), "\n")
#> Individual: 0.438 0.367 0.471
cat("Residual:  ", round(unlist(var_list$Resid), 3), "\n")
#> Residual:   0.241 0.299 0.258

plot_variance_explained()

plot_variance_explained(fit, blocks)

What to look for. Blocks with a large joint fraction are strongly tied to the shared signal; blocks with large individual fractions carry more view-specific biology.

9 Score Plots

plot_scores()

jr <- get_joint_rank(fit)
if (jr >= 2) {
  plot_scores(fit, k = 1, type = "joint", comp_x = 1, comp_y = 2,
              group = meta_df$group)
} else {
  plot_scores(fit, k = 1, type = "joint", comp_x = 1,
              group = meta_df$group)
}

What to look for. Group separation along a score axis suggests the latent component tracks that metadata variable.

plot_components() — "pairs"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "pairs", type = "joint",
                group = meta_df$group)

plot_components() — "density"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "density", type = "joint",
                group = meta_df$group)

What to look for. Bimodal densities suggest discrete sample structure. Unimodal densities point more toward a continuous gradient.

10 AJIVE Diagnostic Plots

plot_components() — "rank_threshold"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "rank_threshold") +
  labs(subtitle = "Threshold = max(Wedin, random-direction) — heuristic, not FWER/FDR-controlled")

What to look for. Squared singular values above the dashed line are called joint. A visible gap around the cutoff is more reassuring than a borderline crossing.

plot_components() — "bound_distributions"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "bound_distributions")

What to look for. Narrow bound distributions give a stable threshold; wide or overlapping distributions suggest the rank decision is fragile.

plot_components() — "ajive_diagnostic" — requires patchwork

if (requireNamespace("patchwork", quietly = TRUE)) {
  plot_components(fit, plot_type = "ajive_diagnostic")
}

What to look for. This is the first diagnostic to inspect. Joint components should clear both cutoffs, and the bound distributions should not make the decision look accidental.

11 Feature Visualization

plot_components() — "top_features"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "top_features", type = "joint",
                block = 1L, component = 1L, top_n = 20L)

What to look for. The top features drive the plotted component most strongly. Treat this as ranking, not as significance.

plot_components() — "component_heatmap"

plot_components(fit, plot_type = "component_heatmap", type = "joint",
                block = 1L)

What to look for. Groups of features with similar loading patterns suggest modules that move together across the component.

12 S3 autoplot / fortify — rajive class

autoplot()

autoplot(fit)

fortify()

tidy_fit <- fortify(fit)
cat("Columns:", names(tidy_fit), "\n")
#> Columns: block type component sample score
head(tidy_fit, 3)
#>   block  type component sample       score
#> 1     1 joint         1      1 -0.12610387
#> 2     1 joint         1      2  0.09265652
#> 3     1 joint         1      3  0.14098029

What to look for. fortify() returns one row per sample per joint component, ready for ggplot2 or dplyr.

13 Feature Ranking Suite

rank_features() — "top_loadings"

top20 <- rank_features(fit, mode = "top_loadings", type = "joint",
                       block = 1L, component = 1L, n = 20L, signed = TRUE)
knitr::kable(head(top20, 10), digits = 4,
             caption = "Top 10 features — Block 1, Joint Component 1")
Top 10 features — Block 1, Joint Component 1
block component type feature_index feature_name loading abs_loading rank
1 1 joint 4 NA -0.3313 0.3313 1
1 1 joint 36 NA -0.2826 0.2826 2
1 1 joint 58 NA 0.2516 0.2516 3
1 1 joint 32 NA 0.2441 0.2441 4
1 1 joint 56 NA 0.2438 0.2438 5
1 1 joint 41 NA 0.2412 0.2412 6
1 1 joint 30 NA 0.2146 0.2146 7
1 1 joint 10 NA -0.2127 0.2127 8
1 1 joint 27 NA -0.2113 0.2113 9
1 1 joint 29 NA -0.1997 0.1997 10

What to look for. High absolute loadings are the strongest component drivers in this block. Use jackstraw_rajive() when the question is statistical significance.

rank_features() — "contribution"

contrib <- rank_features(fit, mode = "contribution", type = "joint",
                         block = 1L, component = 1L)
knitr::kable(head(contrib, 8), digits = 4,
             caption = "Feature contributions (top 8)")
Feature contributions (top 8)
block component type feature_index feature_name loading abs_loading contribution rank
1 1 joint 4 NA -0.3313 0.3313 0.0547 1
1 1 joint 36 NA -0.2826 0.2826 0.0467 2
1 1 joint 58 NA 0.2516 0.2516 0.0415 3
1 1 joint 32 NA 0.2441 0.2441 0.0403 4
1 1 joint 56 NA 0.2438 0.2438 0.0403 5
1 1 joint 41 NA 0.2412 0.2412 0.0398 6
1 1 joint 30 NA 0.2146 0.2146 0.0354 7
1 1 joint 10 NA -0.2127 0.2127 0.0351 8

rank_features() — "overlap"

overlap <- rank_features(fit, mode = "overlap", type = "joint",
                         component = 1L, n = 50L, method = "jaccard")
knitr::kable(overlap, digits = 3,
             caption = "Jaccard overlap of top-50 joint features")
Jaccard overlap of top-50 joint features
component type block_i block_j top_n method n_intersect overlap_score
1 joint 1 2 50 jaccard 40 0.667
1 joint 1 3 50 jaccard 32 0.552
1 joint 2 3 50 jaccard 40 0.800

What to look for. Values near 1 mean the same features dominate both blocks. Moderate overlap is common when different molecular layers share a process without sharing identical feature identities.

Wrappers: get_top_loadings(), get_feature_contributions(), compare_feature_sets_across_blocks()

top_w     <- get_top_loadings(fit, block = 1L, component = 1L, n = 5L)
overlap_w <- compare_feature_sets_across_blocks(fit, component = 1L,
                                                n = 50L, method = "jaccard")
cat("get_top_loadings — first row:\n"); print(top_w[1, ])
#> get_top_loadings — first row:
#>   block component  type feature_index feature_name    loading abs_loading rank
#> 1     1         1 joint             4         <NA> -0.3313351   0.3313351    1
cat("compare_feature_sets_across_blocks:\n"); print(overlap_w)
#> compare_feature_sets_across_blocks:
#>   component  type block_i block_j top_n  method n_intersect overlap_score
#> 1         1 joint       1       2    50 jaccard          40     0.6666667
#> 2         1 joint       1       3    50 jaccard          32     0.5517241
#> 3         1 joint       2       3    50 jaccard          40     0.8000000

14 Association Testing Suite

associate_components() — "categorical"

knitr::kable(assoc_cat, digits = 4,
             caption = "Joint factor — group association (Kruskal-Wallis)")
Joint factor — group association (Kruskal-Wallis)
variable component stat p_value p_adj method
group 1 0.0181 0.8928 0.8928 kruskal
group 2 0.0890 0.7655 0.8928 kruskal

Exploratory-inference caveat. These p-values condition on estimated scores and do not propagate score uncertainty. Treat them as hypothesis-generating.

associate_components() — "continuous"

knitr::kable(assoc_cont, digits = 4,
             caption = "Joint factor — continuous association (Pearson)")
Joint factor — continuous association (Pearson)
variable component stat p_value p_adj method
score_cont 1 0.0108 0.9242 0.9242 pearson
score_cont 2 -0.0898 0.4280 0.8561 pearson

associate_components() — "survival" (requires survival)

assoc_surv <- associate_components(
  fit, meta_df,
  variable   = "time_event",
  mode       = "survival",
  time_col   = "time_event",
  status_col = "event_flag",
  split      = "median"
)
knitr::kable(assoc_surv, digits = 4,
             caption = "Joint factor — survival association (log-rank, median split)")
Joint factor — survival association (log-rank, median split)
variable component stat p_value p_adj method
time_event 1 1.6707 0.1962 0.3923 logrank_median
time_event 2 0.0184 0.8920 0.8920 logrank_median

Survival-split note. The median-split log-rank test is descriptive. For primary inference, prefer split = "none" and the continuous Cox model.

plot_components() — "association"

assoc_all <- rbind(assoc_cat, assoc_cont)
plot_components(assoc_results = assoc_all, plot_type = "association")

Wrappers: associate_scores_continuous(), associate_scores_categorical()

cont_w <- associate_scores_continuous(fit, meta_df, variable = "score_cont")
cat("Same as associate_components(mode='continuous'):\n")
#> Same as associate_components(mode='continuous'):
print(cont_w[1:2, ])
#>     variable component        stat   p_value     p_adj  method
#> 1 score_cont         1  0.01080462 0.9242185 0.9242185 pearson
#> 2 score_cont         2 -0.08984590 0.4280323 0.8560645 pearson

15 Stability Assessment Suite

assess_stability() — "joint_rank"

cat("Bootstrap joint rank distribution (B=5):\n")
#> Bootstrap joint rank distribution (B=5):
print(stab_rank$rank_table)
#> 
#> 2 
#> 5
cat("Observed rank:", stab_rank$observed_rank, "\n")
#> Observed rank: 2

What to look for. A concentrated bootstrap distribution means the selected rank is insensitive to sample resampling.

assess_stability() — "loadings"

for (k in seq_along(stab_load))
  cat(sprintf("  %s: %s\n", names(stab_load)[k],
              paste(round(stab_load[[k]]$cos_similarity, 3), collapse = ", ")))
#>   mRNA: 0.982, 0.962
#>   Methylation: 0.939, 0.993
#>   Proteomics: 0.971, 0.923

Procrustes note. Bootstrap loadings are aligned by orthogonal Procrustes rotation before cosine similarities are computed. Cosine similarity above 0.9 is typically a stable direction.

assess_stability() — "components"

knitr::kable(stab_comp, digits = 3,
             caption = "Bootstrap stability of joint scores (B=5)")
Bootstrap stability of joint scores (B=5)
component mean_correlation sd_correlation
1 0.992 0.004
2 0.918 0.018

plot_stability_heatmap() / plot_components(plot_type = "stability")

plot_stability_heatmap(stab_load)

plot_components(stability_result = stab_load, plot_type = "stability")

Wrappers: bootstrap_joint_rank(), bootstrap_loading_stability()

rank_draws <- bootstrap_joint_rank(blocks = blocks,
                                   initial_signal_ranks = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                                   B = 5L)
load_stab  <- bootstrap_loading_stability(ajive_output = fit,
                                          blocks = blocks,
                                          initial_signal_ranks = c(5L, 4L, 4L),
                                          B = 5L)

16 Summarization Suite

summarize_components() — "components"

knitr::kable(summarize_components(fit, summary_type = "components"),
             caption = "Component catalog")
Component catalog
block type component block_rank joint_rank n_features
1 joint 1 2 2 60
1 joint 2 2 2 60
1 individual 1 3 2 60
1 individual 2 3 2 60
1 individual 3 3 2 60
2 joint 1 2 2 50
2 joint 2 2 2 50
2 individual 1 2 2 50
2 individual 2 2 2 50
3 joint 1 2 2 40
3 joint 2 2 2 40
3 individual 1 2 2 40
3 individual 2 2 2 40

summarize_components() — "variance"

knitr::kable(
  summarize_components(fit, blocks = blocks, summary_type = "variance"),
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Variance decomposition per block"
)
Variance decomposition per block
Joint Indiv Resid
0.321 0.438 0.241
0.334 0.367 0.299
0.271 0.471 0.258

summarize_components() — "associations"

knitr::kable(
  summarize_components(assoc_results = assoc_all, summary_type = "associations"),
  digits = 4,
  caption = "Association summary"
)
Association summary
variable component n_tests min_p_value min_p_adj method
group||1 group 1 1 0.8928 0.8928 kruskal
group||2 group 2 1 0.7655 0.8928 kruskal
score_cont||1 score_cont 1 1 0.9242 0.9242 pearson
score_cont||2 score_cont 2 1 0.4280 0.8561 pearson

summarize_components() — "diagnostics"

knitr::kable(head(summarize_components(fit, summary_type = "diagnostics"), 6),
             digits = 4, caption = "Rank diagnostics (first 6 rows)")
Rank diagnostics (first 6 rows)
component_index obs_sval obs_sval_sq classification joint_rank_estimate overall_sv_sq_threshold wedin_cutoff rand_cutoff perm_cutoff identifiability_norm
1 1.6531 2.7328 joint 2 1.7367 -26.4289 1.7367 NA l2
2 1.6067 2.5815 joint 2 1.7367 -26.4289 1.7367 NA l2
3 1.1718 1.3730 nonjoint 2 1.7367 -26.4289 1.7367 NA l2
4 1.1256 1.2670 nonjoint 2 1.7367 -26.4289 1.7367 NA l2

summarize_components() — "stability"

knitr::kable(
  summarize_components(stability_result = stab_rank, summary_type = "stability"),
  digits = 3, caption = "Bootstrap joint rank distribution"
)
Bootstrap joint rank distribution
joint_rank frequency proportion
2 5 1

summarize_components() — "significance"

knitr::kable(
  summarize_components(jackstraw_result = js, summary_type = "significance"),
  digits = 4, caption = "Significant features per block x component"
)
Significant features per block x component
block component n_features n_significant prop_significant min_p_value min_p_adj
1 1 1 1 60 39 0.6500 0.0017 0.0055
1 2 1 2 60 35 0.5833 0.0017 0.0055
2 1 2 1 50 31 0.6200 0.0020 0.0055
2 2 2 2 50 25 0.5000 0.0020 0.0055
3 1 3 1 40 28 0.7000 0.0025 0.0055
3 2 3 2 40 23 0.5750 0.0025 0.0055

17 Jackstraw Significance Testing

jackstraw_rajive()

set.seed(42)
js <- jackstraw_rajive(
  ajive_output = fit,
  blocks       = blocks,
  alpha        = 0.05,
  n_null       = 5L,       # use >= 100 in practice
  correction   = "BH"
)

Significance note. The jackstraw identifies features associated with the estimated joint score. It does not establish causal drivers, and the decomposition is not re-estimated after feature masking.

print.jackstraw_rajive() / summary.jackstraw_rajive()

print(js)
#> JIVE Jackstraw Significance Test
#>   Joint rank: 2   Alpha: 0.05   Correction: BH
#> 
#>   Block      Component    N features     N significant 
#>   ----------------------------------------------------
#>   block1     comp1        60             39            
#>   block1     comp2        60             35            
#>   block2     comp1        50             31            
#>   block2     comp2        50             25            
#>   block3     comp1        40             28            
#>   block3     comp2        40             23
summary(js)
#>   block component n_features n_significant alpha correction
#>  block1     comp1         60            39  0.05         BH
#>  block1     comp2         60            35  0.05         BH
#>  block2     comp1         50            31  0.05         BH
#>  block2     comp2         50            25  0.05         BH
#>  block3     comp1         40            28  0.05         BH
#>  block3     comp2         40            23  0.05         BH

autoplot.jackstraw_rajive() / fortify.jackstraw_rajive()

autoplot(js)

tidy_js <- fortify(js)
cat("Columns:", names(tidy_js), "\n")
#> Columns: block component feature_index p_value p_adj significant
head(tidy_js, 4)
#>    block component feature_index     p_value       p_adj significant
#> 1      1         1             1 0.001663894 0.005500953        TRUE
#> 4      1         1             4 0.001663894 0.005500953        TRUE
#> 5      1         1             5 0.001663894 0.005500953        TRUE
#> 10     1         1            10 0.001663894 0.005500953        TRUE

plot_jackstraw() — "pvalue_hist"

plot_jackstraw(js, type = "pvalue_hist", block = 1L, component = 1L)

What to look for. A spike near zero followed by a near-uniform tail suggests real signal plus calibrated null features. A fully uniform histogram suggests little feature-level signal for that component.

plot_jackstraw() — "scatter"

plot_jackstraw(js, type = "scatter", block = 1L, component = 1L,
               label_top = 5L)

plot_jackstraw() — "loadings_significance"

plot_jackstraw(js, type = "loadings_significance", block = 1L)

plot_components() — "volcano" / "jackstraw_summary"

plot_components(jackstraw_result = js, plot_type = "volcano",
                block = 1L, component = 1L)

plot_components(jackstraw_result = js, plot_type = "jackstraw_summary")

get_significant_vars() / summarize_significant_vars()

sig_b1c1 <- get_significant_vars(js, block = 1L, component = 1L)
n_sig_b1c1 <- if (is.data.frame(sig_b1c1)) nrow(sig_b1c1) else 0L
cat("Significant features (block 1, component 1):", n_sig_b1c1, "\n")
#> Significant features (block 1, component 1): 0
if (isTRUE(n_sig_b1c1 > 0L))
  knitr::kable(head(sig_b1c1, 8), digits = 4)
sig_all <- summarize_significant_vars(js)
if (is.data.frame(sig_all) && nrow(sig_all) > 0L) {
  knitr::kable(head(sig_all, 8), digits = 4,
               caption = "All significant features (all blocks x components)")
} else {
  knitr::kable(tibble::tibble(message = "No significant features at the current threshold."))
}
All significant features (all blocks x components)
block component feature_index p_value p_adj significant
1 1 1 1 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
4 1 1 4 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
5 1 1 5 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
10 1 1 10 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
12 1 1 12 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
16 1 1 16 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
21 1 1 21 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE
22 1 1 22 0.0017 0.0055 TRUE

18 Reporting and Export

export_results() — "csv" / "rds"

top_feats <- rank_features(fit, mode = "top_loadings", type = "joint",
                           block = 1L, component = 1L, n = 20L)
tmp_csv <- tempfile(fileext = ".csv")
export_results(top_feats, path = tmp_csv, format = "csv")
rt <- read.csv(tmp_csv)
cat("Round-trip rows/cols:", nrow(rt), "/", ncol(rt), "\n")
#> Round-trip rows/cols: 20 / 8
tmp_rds <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds")
export_results(fit, path = tmp_rds, format = "rds")
fit2 <- readRDS(tmp_rds)
cat("Reloaded joint rank:", get_joint_rank(fit2), "\n")
#> Reloaded joint rank: 2

rajive_report()

tmp_html <- tempfile(fileext = ".html")
rajive_report(
  ajive_output     = fit,
  blocks           = blocks,
  metadata         = meta_df,
  jackstraw_result = js,
  output_file      = tmp_html,
  sections         = c("overview", "variance", "features",
                       "associations", "stability")
)
cat("Report written to:", tmp_html, "\n")
#> Report written to: /tmp/RtmpdzkyT6/file8ba497b8002d5.html
cat("File size (bytes):", file.info(tmp_html)$size, "\n")
#> File size (bytes): 6346

What to look for. The report is a portable review artifact. For final analysis decisions, use the specialized tables and plots shown above.

19 Appendix — Wrapper Quick Reference

Wrapper Equivalent underlying call
get_top_loadings(fit, block=1, component=1) rank_features(fit, mode="top_loadings", block=1, component=1)
get_feature_contributions(fit, block=1) rank_features(fit, mode="contribution", block=1)
compare_feature_sets_across_blocks(fit, n=50) rank_features(fit, mode="overlap", n=50)
summarize_significant_vars(js) rank_features(jackstraw_result=js, mode="significant")
associate_scores_continuous(fit, meta, variable="x") associate_components(fit, meta, variable="x", mode="continuous")
associate_scores_categorical(fit, meta, variable="g") associate_components(fit, meta, variable="g", mode="categorical")
associate_scores_survival(fit, meta, time_col="t", status_col="e") associate_components(fit, meta, mode="survival", time_col="t", status_col="e")
bootstrap_joint_rank(blocks=blocks, initial_signal_ranks=ranks, B=5) assess_stability(blocks=blocks, initial_signal_ranks=ranks, target="joint_rank", B=5)
bootstrap_loading_stability(fit, blocks, ranks, B=5) assess_stability(fit, blocks, ranks, target="loadings", B=5)
plot_stability_heatmap(stab_load) plot_components(stability_result=stab_load, plot_type="stability")
